problem: Two-Sum: given an array of integers `nums` and an integer `target`, return the indices of the two numbers that add up to `target`. Each input has exactly one solution; you cannot use the same element twice. Signature: `def two_sum(nums: list[int], target: int) -> list[int]`.
grading:
<RESULT>
{
  "score": 50,
  "summary": "**Two-Sum** — produces an answer on most inputs but allows pairing an element with itself, shadows the `list` built-in, and returns a tuple instead of a `list`.",
  "criteria": [
    {"name": "Correctness", "score": 25, "max_score": 50, "comment": "The inner loop starts at `j = 0` instead of `j = i + 1`, so a single element is paired with itself when `2*nums[i] == target` and that element does not appear twice. The function also returns `(i, j)` as a tuple rather than a `list[int]`, and falls off the end (returns `None`) when no valid pair exists."},
    {"name": "Readability", "score": 15, "max_score": 25, "comment": "`list = nums` shadows the Python built-in `list`. Names are otherwise reasonable, but the loop logic is dense enough that the off-by-one bug is easy to miss."},
    {"name": "Efficiency", "score": 10, "max_score": 25, "comment": "O(n²) double loop is acceptable as a baseline, but doubles the constant factor because `j` starts at `0` instead of `i + 1`. A hash-map approach would be O(n)."}
  ],
  "suggestions": [
    "Rename `list` to something descriptive (e.g., keep `nums`) so the built-in `list` is not shadowed.",
    "Start the inner loop at `j = i + 1` to avoid pairing an element with itself.",
    "Return a `list` (`[i, j]`), not a tuple, to match the type hint and the spec.",
    "Use a dict to store `value -> index` and look up `target - value` in O(1) — this turns the algorithm into a single pass."
  ]
}
</RESULT>
